name: Pueblos de Tierra de Alba  — circular desde San Miguel de Serrezuela
region_intro: Road cycling routes around San Miguel De Serrezuela traverse a landscape characterized by significant elevation changes and mountain passes. The region features a mix of open terrain and routes that connect various villages. Road cyclists can expect challenging climbs and descents, with several routes incorporating named passes. The area offers diverse terrain suitable for experienced riders. Best road cycling routes around San Miguel De Serrezuela The most popular road cycling route is Pueblos de Tierra de Alba — circular desde San Miguel de Serrezuela, a 61.4 miles (98.8 km) trail that takes 4 hours 21 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the villages of the Tierra de Alba region. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Puerto de Villatoro – Las Fuentes Pass loop from Martínez, a difficult 62.8 miles (101.0 km) path. This route features significant climbs over mountain passes, offering extensive views. Local road cyclists also love the Fuente del Oro – Mancera de Arriba loop from Cabezas del Villar, a 43.7 miles (70.4 km) trail leading through rural landscapes, often completed in about 3 hours 4 minutes. Road cycling around San Miguel De Serrezuela is defined by challenging mountain passes, rolling hills, and connections between local villages. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 60 road cyclists have used komoot to explore San Miguel De Serrezuela's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around San Miguel De Serrezuela?
A: There are 11 road cycling routes documented on komoot for the San Miguel De Serrezuela area. These routes offer a range of difficulties, from easy to difficult, catering to various experience levels.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes for beginners in San Miguel De Serrezuela?
A: While the region is known for its challenging terrain, there is at least one easy road cycling route available. Most routes, however, feature significant elevation changes and are better suited for experienced riders.

Q: What makes road cycling in San Miguel De Serrezuela challenging?
A: Road cycling in San Miguel De Serrezuela is characterized by significant elevation changes, including challenging climbs over mountain passes and subsequent descents. The terrain often features rolling hills and connects various villages, providing a demanding yet rewarding experience for experienced cyclists.

Q: What is the typical length of road cycling routes in this region?
A: The road cycling routes around San Miguel De Serrezuela vary in length. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1063066800/pueblos-de-tierra-de-alba-circular-desde-san-miguel-de-serrezuela" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pueblos de Tierra de Alba — circular desde San Miguel de Serrezuela&lt;/a&gt; route is approximately 61.4 miles (98.8 km), while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20141646" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuente del Oro – Mancera de Arriba loop from Cabezas del Villar&lt;/a&gt; is about 43.7 miles (70.4 km).

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, several routes are designed as loops. A notable example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1063066800/pueblos-de-tierra-de-alba-circular-desde-san-miguel-de-serrezuela" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pueblos de Tierra de Alba — circular desde San Miguel de Serrezuela&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a comprehensive tour of the region's villages.

Q: When is the best time of year for road cycling in San Miguel De Serrezuela?
A: Given the region's significant elevation changes and mountain passes, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling, with milder temperatures. Summer can be quite warm, especially at lower elevations, while winter may bring colder temperatures and potential for snow at higher altitudes.

Q: What kind of scenery can I expect on the road cycling routes?
A: The routes offer diverse scenery, ranging from open terrain to rural landscapes connecting various villages. You can expect extensive views, especially from mountain passes like those featured in the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5316323" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Villatoro – Las Fuentes Pass loop from Martínez&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any less crowded road cycling routes in the area?
A: Many of the routes traverse rural landscapes and connect smaller villages, which often means less traffic compared to major roads. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20141646" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuente del Oro – Mancera de Arriba loop from Cabezas del Villar&lt;/a&gt; offer a chance to explore quieter country roads.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in San Miguel De Serrezuela?
A: Over 60 road cyclists have explored this region using komoot. They often appreciate the challenging nature of the routes, the extensive views from mountain passes, and the opportunity to connect between local villages, experiencing the diverse terrain.

Q: Are there routes that feature specific mountain passes?
A: Yes, several routes incorporate well-known mountain passes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5316323" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Villatoro – Las Fuentes Pass loop from Martínez&lt;/a&gt; includes significant climbs over mountain passes, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38156967" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Peña Negra – Peña Negra Pass loop from Diego Álvaro&lt;/a&gt; specifically highlights the Peña Negra Pass.

Q: Can I find routes that connect different villages?
A: Connecting villages is a defining characteristic of road cycling in this region. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1063066800/pueblos-de-tierra-de-alba-circular-desde-san-miguel-de-serrezuela" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pueblos de Tierra de Alba — circular desde San Miguel de Serrezuela&lt;/a&gt; are designed to explore multiple settlements, offering a cultural and scenic journey.
tips: A place in the countryside surrounded by holm oaks. Every year on Pentecost Sunday, a pilgrimage is held where hundreds of people come to the festival next to the hermitage.
Arriving at the town of Galinduste you can see some immense fields of cultivation that in rainy seasons look green. In addition, these fields are interchanged with cork oak meadows.

Ejeme is a municipality in the province of Salamanca, in the Autonomous Community of Castilla y León, Spain. It is at a distance of 27 km from Salamanca, the provincial capital.
It belongs to the Tierra de Alba region, Salamanca judicial district. Its municipal term is formed by Martín Vicente and Portillo.

Pedrosillo de Alba is located about 11 km. From Alba de Tormes. There are hardly any data on its historical evolution, although this must be related to that of its county seat. The parish church of San Pedro stands free-standing at one end of the town.

waypoints.name: Parish church of San Pedro in Pedrosillo de Alba
